behaviors.description: The review input is scoped to the lines that changed, not the full content of each modified file.
behaviors.justification: A pure unit test with mocked diffs would never catch if the real diff parsing/filtering logic is broken. For example, the code might incorrectly include unchanged lines if the diff parser mis-handles context lines or whitespace. The failure is in the boundary between raw diff format and analyzed lines, which requires parsing actual diff structure (unified format with hunks, context, etc.). This crosses a component boundary between diff parser and analyzer.
behaviors.test_description: Load a real PR diff (e.g., unified diff format with multiple changed hunks, unchanged lines, and whitespace variations) into the system; verify that only the exact lines flagged as changed in the diff are passed to the analysis component, not full file content or extra context lines.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are attached to the correct positions in the diff view on the pull request.
behaviors.justification: GitHub's API enforces a hard rule: comments can only be placed on lines that are part of the diff (added/removed/context). A typical mock API would accept any line number without enforcing this constraint, hiding off-by-one errors or line number calculation bugs. The real GitHub API will reject or misplace comments at wrong line positions. This failure is only visible with real GitHub interaction, not an in-process mock, because the validation rule lives in GitHub's server, not the client.
behaviors.test_description: Create or use a real test GitHub PR with a multi-file diff. Post review comments at various positions (start of diff, middle of hunks, context lines, boundaries). Verify each comment lands at the correct line in the GitHub web diff view, respects commentable-line constraints, and fails appropriately if attempting to comment on non-commentable lines.

behaviors.description: The agent identifies at least one real defect in a pull request that contains a known introduced bug.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests the core capability of the LLM-based agent to detect bugs in code. A mocked LLM that returns hardcoded findings (e.g., 'always returns a bug at line 5') would always pass the test, masking failures where the real LLM fails to spot the defect due to prompt tuning, training data gaps, or the model's inherent limitations. The failure is non-deterministic and depends on what the real LLM actually infers about code quality. This is the definition of Agentic-level failure.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR containing a known, subtle bug (e.g., uncaught exception path, missing input validation, race condition, or logic error that static analysis would miss). Run the real agent with a real LLM against this PR. Verify the agent's findings include identification of at least this one defect.

behaviors.description: Every finding listed in the top-level summary corresponds to an inline comment that was also posted — no findings appear in the summary without a matching inline comment.
behaviors.justification: The failure is divergence between two code paths: one that generates inline comments and another that generates the summary. A unit test mocking each path separately would never catch if they diverge when handling real, complex findings (e.g., findings with variable lengths, special characters, or truncation). Testing them in isolation with contrived inputs hides real divergence. Running the full pipeline with realistic findings (from mocked but varied LLM output) and verifying consistency across both posting paths requires Integration level.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full analysis pipeline on a test PR with multiple diverse findings: some short, some long, some with code blocks, special characters, or markdown. Verify that every finding in the final summary comment has a corresponding inline comment posted with matching content, and vice versa, with no orphaned findings in either direction.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — reading the diff, posting inline comments, posting the summary comment — completes correctly for a real pull request without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: The workflow includes: reading a real GitHub PR (System), analyzing code with the real LLM (Agentic), and posting comments to GitHub (System). The most distinctive failure modes are those involving LLM non-determinism (analysis timeout, hallucination, refusing to analyze), which require Agentic level testing. While System (real GitHub posting) is also required, Agentic is the higher/binding constraint. Workflow would only be necessary if multiple independent agents were chained in sequence; here there is one agent with one agentic analysis step.
behaviors.test_description: Run the complete PR review workflow end-to-end on a real test GitHub PR: fetch the diff, invoke the LLM-based analyzer, post all inline review comments, and post the top-level summary comment. Verify all steps complete successfully without hangs, crashes, or partial failures requiring manual recovery. Check that the summary and comments are visible on GitHub afterward.
